result:
I was walking under the interstate at dusk when the word “silhouette” first felt complete. One of those pop-up thunderstorms had rinsed the city, and every surface collected the last blue of the sky the way cloth collects a scent. A man sat against a concrete buttress, hood cinched so tight only his cigarette showed, an orange punctuation mark suspended in mid-air. Behind him the freeway roared like an ocean kept in a sound-effects can, and around us the underbelly of overpasses curved upward like the ribs of something impossible that had decided to rest right here, on the edge of the river district. I smelled wet grass warming back up, diesel, tomato paste drifting over from the cannery. All of it said: evening.  

I was thinking about translation again—how the world keeps handing you closed objects and whispers “interpret.” The orange ember translated the man into a tiny, brilliant statement of survival, and the steady roar above us translated motion into noise, speed into standing still. I had come down here because some friend with a misplaced sense of poetry was worried about a colony of kudzu that was, in her words, “escaping from the south side.” Kudzu being the most literal minded traveler in the kingdom of metaphor, it had taken one look at the corrugated gray shadows and believed them a continuation of the Carolina hills. We were, all of us, emigrants here.  

Before I found the vine, I found the river slipping sideways under a train trestle, and I found it was speaking in two languages at once. In daylight English it was an industrial artery, barges nudging crushed scrap like burly shoppers pushing carts. But in blue-hour Latin it turned into pater noster, the father of us all, winding back to the glacial teeth that bit it out. I was reminded that rivers start as omissions—places where the ground gives up. That sentence went into my memory the way coins sink in a wishing well: noiselessly, weighted with want.  

By the time I reached the kudzu patch, the sky had smoothed to one panel of lapis. Street lamps hadn’t yet blinked on, which made the vine look phosphorescent, a green animal dreaming it is a building. The leaves were still wet, heart-shaped, and every heart clapped gently against its neighbor. I stood close enough to hear it—a hush hush—as though the plant applauded the dark. I remembered my grandmother warning me that kudzu will swallow barns whole, and I thought, let it. Let it swallow the rust, the hollowed-out warehouses, the parking meters that blink EEEEE because nobody feeds them coins anymore. Let it swallow every billboard crying out for new phones. Let it swallow, too, the ache in my hips that arrives at thirty-eight like a bill collector.  

Somewhere above, a lone gull crossed the seam where concrete met cloud. I followed its trajectory as if my own life depended on gradient and lift. Why does the bright edge of anything—the wing tip, the ember, the last shard of daylight—feel truer than the voluminous center? My inbox was full of messages I would never answer in their proper tongues, but maybe this particulate blue could do the work for me: I’m not ignoring you; I’m learning the native grammar of almost.  

I took out my phone, not to photograph but to record a voice memo. Predictably, nothing came. Instead, I played back random bits I’d stored months ago: an orchestra tuning on a Brussels sidewalk, the escalator at the medical center advising calm, the long static hush of an MRI machine. Each was a container I’d wanted, at the time, to mail to a friend who was drowning in her own silence. But because listening to someone else’s sound is like borrowing their nerve endings, I’d never sent the files. They remained in their little folders, miracles lacking the etiquette of gifts.  

Wind changed direction. The man by the wall coughed twice, stubbed the cigarette on the sole of his sneaker, and began to sing. The melody rolled over the syllables he chose as if language itself were river, vine, and cloud, and suddenly I could hear a past that didn’t quite belong to me—Saturday nights on somebody’s porch, cornmeal thrown to chickens, a woman running a damp rag along a child’s hairline. I wanted to fold the tune into the same pocket where I keep train schedules and tides tables, never quite convenient but always arriving precisely when the body has a ripple it cannot translate into sense.  

Eventually, streetlamps came on, but not all at once—like people remembering their own names at staggered intervals. Light found the pockmarks in the concrete’s skin, the graffiti tag that began in exuberant copperplate and petered out into lowercase apology. The effect was of a document worn away by devotion. I thought about how every surface becomes preamble to the next layer of ache, whether that layer goes by the name paint, rust, grief, or moss. The kudzu shimmered and exhaled; somewhere inside its tendrils a cell made the microscopic decision to wrap one more millimeter around a lamppost. The moment almost clicked audibly, like beads counting on an abacus built for the obscure mathematics of persistence.  

Yes, the world is weary of us. Still, it keeps rehearsing small enchantments. Consider the way warm air rises off asphalt after rain and carries the smell of gasoline cut with petrichor—that hybrid perfume mixing progress and ruin until both finally admit they are the same secret. Or the way my heartbeat aligns, four-to-the-bar, with the guy’s half-remembered spiritual. I have no claim to this underpass, this state, or even the reliable tomorrow my phone predicts in optimistic sans serif. What I have is the crackle in his voice when it breaks at the hinge of baritone and falsetto. I take it like water cupped between two scarred hands.  

I walked back through corridors of lilac shadow. Each step triggered motion sensors in derelict doorways, and every time a rectangle of white burst onto a brick wall I felt accused by the word evidence. Still, I carried on, because going forward is only a more polite way of circling back. Eventually the sidewalk climbed the ramp to the city proper, where taco stands exhaled lime steam and rideshare drivers leaned on hoods telling jokes in languages that braid together mid-laugh. I crossed the avenue during a gap in traffic and thought something I didn’t say aloud: This is the agreement. We soften the margins of damage for each other. We hand over what small, bright, portable thing we can. Maybe tomorrow the kudzu will have covered the man’s cigarette butt and the abutment both, and only the orange color will remain in the deep folds, fibrous memory of combustion. 

Inside my apartment, I shut the door like a sentence ending with a comma. I took off my shoes and arranged them on the rug—two dark accents awaiting their next use. I opened the refrigerator, stared into its small and humdrum constellation, and closed it without choosing. I scrolled to the voice memo titled simply 9:36 and hit play. The gull cried above the freeway again, the man sang again, the river promised again that it would get somewhere. Somewhere I am already inside, already outside. Somewhere I keep practicing the imperfect translation of caring.  

I fell asleep with all the lights on, as if a well-lit room could prevent the night from devouring its own edges. Somewhere before dawn, the power flashed out. When I woke, the apartment was the color of fossils. My phone had no charge, so the city could not find me. I opened the window. Birds whose names I had never bothered to ask were speaking the language of new angles, and I understood them almost perfectly.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
